resources.string: Extended history, widgets, exports, and throttling insights are active.
Not enough data for this metric yet
Allow location permission to show the current WiFi network name.
Turn on device location to let Android share the current WiFi network name.
Android is not currently exposing the WiFi network name to the app.
Allow media access for storage tools
Grant media access only if you want accurate photos, videos, audio, downloads, and cleanup suggestions. runcheck uses this data only on your device.
Could not delete the selected files.
Android blocked deletion for one or more files.
Runs approximately on this cadence. Doze and battery optimizations may delay background checks.
Alerts for low battery, high temperature, low storage
Send anonymous crash diagnostics to Firebase Crashlytics to help fix bugs.
Choose how long runcheck keeps your history on this device.
Free keeps the last 24 hours of history. Upgrade to pro to keep 3 months, 6 months, 1 year, or everything.
No pro purchase found for this Play account
Play Billing is not ready yet. Try again in a moment.
Play Billing is unavailable on this device. Upgrade options are hidden until billing becomes available.
Payment is being processed. Pro will unlock when complete.
Purchase could not be completed. Please try again.
Google Play Billing is still starting up. Try again in a moment.
Google Play Billing is temporarily unavailable. Try again shortly.
Could not reach Google Play. Check your connection and try again.
This purchase is not available right now.
You already own runcheck Pro. Restoring now.
Alert when storage is almost full
This will permanently delete all monitoring history. This cannot be undone.
Upgrade to runcheck pro to use %1$s.
Week, month, and all-time battery history require runcheck pro.
Thermal throttling history requires runcheck pro.
Storage cleanup tools require runcheck pro.
Signal history and extended speed test data require runcheck pro.
Shows battery level, temperature, and current
Shows overall health score with category indicators
Alerts for battery, temperature, and storage issues
Foreground service used only while the user is actively viewing real-time device health metrics that require continuous updates.
Add chargers here, then select one before you start charging to save comparison data.
This will delete %1$s and its saved test history.
Average result from completed charger tests.
Choose a saved charger before charging so Runcheck can log the next session to the right profile.
The current charging session is being logged to this charger.
The next charging session will be saved to this charger.
No usage data available for the last 24 hours
This can happen on a fresh install or right after granting usage access. Give Android a moment, then retry.
Android requires Usage Access permission before runcheck can show per-app foreground time.
Tap the ring to start a speed test
Tap the ring to run another test
Tap the ring to try again
Connect to WiFi or cellular to run a speed test
Could not reach the speed test server.
The speed test timed out. Try again.
Unable to complete the speed test.
No saved speed tests yet. Run a test to start building history.
This test will use approximately 20–50 MB of mobile data
Speed test powered by M-Lab. Test data contributes to open internet research.
One-time purchase. No subscription. Pay once, own it forever.
All Pro features are now unlocked. Enjoy the full runcheck experience.
All Pro features are unlocked for 7 days. Explore everything.
Your battery history now has 5 days of data. Keep tracking with Pro.
Extended history, charger comparison, and exports are now locked. Ads will appear on detail screens.
Your Pro trial ended. Unlock Pro to keep all features.
Your Pro trial ended. Unlock Pro for %1$s.
Reminders about your Pro trial status
Your Pro trial has 2 days left. Keep tracking with Pro.
Your 7-day Pro trial is over. Unlock Pro to keep all features.
Voltage is how much electrical pressure your battery is producing right now. Think of it like water pressure in a pipe.
3.7–4.2 V is healthy. Below 3.5 V under normal use may signal a worn battery.
Erratic voltage causes unexpected shutdowns and inaccurate battery percentage readings.

A lithium-ion cell has a nominal voltage of ~3.7 V. It charges to 4.2 V and is considered empty around 3.0 V. The percentage your phone shows is a mapping from this voltage curve, adjusted for battery age.
How hot your battery is right now. Batteries generate heat during use and charging.
20–35 °C is normal. Above 40 °C during regular use is concerning.
Sustained high temperatures permanently degrade battery capacity. Every degree above 30 °C accelerates chemical aging.
Lithium-ion batteries degrade faster at high temperatures due to accelerated electrolyte decomposition. Storing or operating a phone at 45 °C can double the rate of capacity loss compared to 25 °C.
An overall assessment of your battery\'s condition based on its capacity relative to when it was new.
Good or better means your battery is performing well. Fair or Poor means noticeable degradation.
A degraded battery holds less charge, which means shorter usage between charges and potentially unexpected shutdowns.
How many full charge-discharge cycles your battery has completed. Using 50% and recharging twice counts as one cycle.
Most phone batteries are rated for 500–800 cycles before significant degradation.
Higher cycle counts correlate with reduced battery capacity. This is a key indicator of battery wear.
Modern lithium-ion batteries retain about 80% of their original capacity after 500 cycles under ideal conditions. Fast charging, heat, and deep discharges can reduce this number.
The estimated percentage of your battery\'s original capacity that remains. 100% means like-new.
90–100% is excellent. 80–90% is normal after 1–2 years. Below 80% means noticeable degradation.
This directly tells you how much of the original battery capacity is available. Below 80%, you\'ll notice significantly shorter battery life.
The amount of electrical charge your battery can hold, measured in milliamp-hours (mAh). Compares the design capacity to estimated current capacity.
Varies by device. A typical phone battery is 3000–5000 mAh when new.
Lower capacity means less usage time between charges. The gap between design and estimated capacity shows how much your battery has worn.
How much electrical current is flowing in or out of your battery right now. Positive means charging, negative means discharging.
Discharging: -100 to -800 mA is typical. Charging: 500–3000 mA depending on charger.
High discharge current means your phone is working hard and will drain faster. During charging, higher current means faster charging but more heat.
The accuracy of current readings varies by device manufacturer. Some phones report instantaneous current, others report a rolling average. The min/avg/max stats help smooth out momentary spikes.
How much power your battery is consuming or receiving, calculated from voltage × current. Watts give a more accurate picture than mA alone.
Typical discharge: 1–5 W. Charging: 5–25 W depending on charger and phone support.
Power consumption directly relates to how fast your battery drains. Higher wattage during charging means faster charge times but also more heat generation.
How fast your battery percentage is dropping, measured as percent per hour.
0.5–2%/h is normal for light use. 3–5%/h is moderate. Above 5%/h suggests heavy use or a problem.
This is the simplest indicator of how fast your phone is using battery. If it\'s consistently high while idle, something may be running in the background.
How reliable runcheck considers the battery readings from your device. Not all phones report accurate current and power data.
Green (Accurate) means readings match expected patterns. Yellow (Estimated) means values are derived or less certain.
If confidence is low, take current and power readings as approximate. Level and temperature readings are almost always reliable.
Compares how fast your battery drains with the screen on versus off. The screen is one of the biggest power consumers.
Screen-off drain should be much lower than screen-on. If screen-off drain is above 2%/h, something is keeping your phone awake.
Large screen-off drain usually means a background app or service is consuming power. This helps identify hidden battery drainers.
Tracks whether your phone enters deep sleep (Doze mode) when the screen is off, or stays awake processing tasks.
A healthy phone should spend most of its screen-off time in deep sleep. High \'held awake\' time means something is preventing sleep.
Deep sleep dramatically reduces battery drain. If your phone is held awake for extended periods, background apps or wake locks are likely the cause.
The current temperature of your phone\'s processor. The CPU heats up during intensive tasks like gaming or video editing.
30–45 °C is normal under load. Above 50 °C means heavy thermal stress.
When the CPU gets too hot, your phone will slow itself down (thermal throttling) to prevent damage. This directly affects performance.

CPU temperature is read from system thermal zone files. Not all devices expose this data, and the reported zone may vary. On some devices, this reflects the skin temperature rather than the CPU die temperature.
How much room your phone has before it starts throttling performance to cool down. Lower numbers mean closer to throttling.
Above 0.5 is comfortable. 0.2–0.5 means getting warm. Below 0.2 means throttling is likely imminent.
This is the best predictor of when your phone will slow down. If headroom drops near zero, expect reduced performance until the device cools.

Thermal headroom is reported by Android\'s PowerManager API (API 30+). It represents a normalized value where values approaching the throttling threshold indicate imminent performance reduction. The OS uses multiple thermal sensors to calculate this value.
The overall thermal state your device has reported to Android. Ranges from none (cool) to critical (emergency cooling needed).
None or Light is normal. Moderate means noticeable throttling. Severe or higher needs cooldown.
Higher thermal status levels trigger increasingly aggressive throttling — first background tasks slow, then GPU, then CPU. Critical status may trigger an emergency shutdown.
Whether your phone is currently reducing its performance to control temperature. Active throttling means your phone is intentionally running slower.
No throttling is ideal. Brief throttling during heavy workloads is normal. Persistent throttling at idle suggests a hardware or software issue.
Throttling directly impacts your user experience — apps open slower, games drop frames, and tasks take longer to complete.
How strong the wireless signal is at your location, measured in dBm (decibels relative to one milliwatt). More negative means weaker.
Wi-Fi: -30 to -50 dBm is excellent, -70 to -80 dBm is weak. Cellular: -80 to -100 dBm is good, below -110 dBm is poor.
Weak signal means slower speeds, more dropped connections, and higher battery drain as your phone works harder to maintain the connection.

Signal strength in dBm is logarithmic — every 3 dBm change doubles or halves the power. A -80 dBm signal is 10 times weaker than -70 dBm. Your phone dynamically adjusts transmission power based on signal quality.
How long it takes for data to travel from your phone to a server and back, measured in milliseconds. Lower is better.
Under 20 ms is excellent. 20–50 ms is good for most uses. Above 100 ms is noticeable in video calls and gaming.
High latency makes real-time activities feel sluggish — video calls lag, online games stutter, and web pages feel slow to respond even with fast download speeds.
How much your latency varies from one packet to the next. High jitter means an inconsistent connection even if average speed is good.
Under 5 ms is excellent. 5–30 ms is acceptable. Above 30 ms can cause quality issues in calls and streaming.
Jitter causes audio dropouts, video freezing, and buffering. A connection with low average latency but high jitter can be worse than a slightly slower but stable connection.
The radio band your Wi-Fi connection is using. 2.4 GHz has longer range but slower speeds. 5 GHz and 6 GHz are faster but shorter range.
2.4 GHz: up to ~150 Mbps. 5 GHz: up to ~1 Gbps. 6 GHz: up to ~2 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6E).
Choosing the right band can significantly improve your experience. Use 5/6 GHz near the router for speed, or 2.4 GHz for range through walls.
The Wi-Fi protocol version your connection is using. Newer standards support faster speeds and better handling of multiple devices.
Wi-Fi 4 (802.11n) is basic. Wi-Fi 5 (802.11ac) is good. Wi-Fi 6/6E (802.11ax) is latest and fastest.
Both your phone and router must support the same standard. Connecting via an older standard limits your maximum speed regardless of your internet plan.
The theoretical maximum speed of your current Wi-Fi connection, negotiated between your phone and router.
Depends on your Wi-Fi standard and signal. 72 Mbps on Wi-Fi 4, 433+ Mbps on Wi-Fi 5, 1200+ Mbps on Wi-Fi 6.
Actual speeds are always lower than link speed due to overhead, interference, and distance. But a low link speed caps your maximum possible throughput.
The channel width your Wi-Fi connection is using. Wider channels allow more data to flow but are more susceptible to interference.
20 MHz is basic. 40 MHz is common on 2.4 GHz. 80 MHz and 160 MHz are used on 5/6 GHz for high speeds.
Wider channels deliver faster speeds in ideal conditions but may perform worse in crowded Wi-Fi environments where many networks overlap.
Maximum Transmission Unit — the largest packet size your connection can send without fragmenting. Usually 1500 bytes for Ethernet/Wi-Fi.
1500 is standard. VPN connections often use lower values (1400–1450). Values below 1400 may indicate tunneling.
Incorrect MTU can cause mysterious connection issues — pages that partially load, downloads that stall, or VPN tunnels that fail.
The percentage of your phone\'s total storage that is currently occupied by apps, media, and system data.
Under 75% is comfortable. 75–90% is getting full. Above 90% may cause performance issues.
When storage is nearly full, your phone can\'t create temporary files efficiently, which slows down apps, updates, and even basic operations like taking photos.
How fast your storage is filling up, calculated from usage trends over the past week.
Under 100 MB/day is typical. Large app installs or photo shoots cause temporary spikes.
A consistently high fill rate means you\'ll need to clean up sooner. This helps predict when you might run out of space.
Temporary data stored by apps to speed up loading times. Includes downloaded images, compiled code, and offline data.
1–5 GB is typical. Social media and streaming apps tend to accumulate the most cache.
Clearing cache frees space but apps will reload data from the internet, which may briefly slow them down. It\'s safe to clear and can recover significant space.
The total storage used by all installed applications, including their data and cache.
Varies widely. 20–50 GB is common on a well-used phone. Games can use 2–10 GB each.
Apps are often the largest storage consumers. Uninstalling unused apps is one of the most effective ways to reclaim space.
How fast your connection can receive data, measured in Megabits per second (Mbps). This affects streaming, browsing, and downloading files.
10–50 Mbps is good for most uses. 100+ Mbps is excellent. Under 5 Mbps may struggle with video streaming.
Download speed determines how quickly content loads — web pages, videos, app updates, and files. Most daily activities depend on download speed.
How fast your connection can send data. This affects video calls, uploading photos, and sending large files.
5–20 Mbps is good. 50+ Mbps is excellent. Under 2 Mbps may cause video call quality issues.
Upload speed is critical for video calls, livestreaming, cloud backups, and sharing content. It\'s typically lower than download speed.
The round-trip time for data to travel to the test server and back. Same as latency — lower is better.
Under 20 ms is excellent. 20–50 ms is good. Above 100 ms is noticeable lag.
Low ping is essential for real-time activities like gaming and video calls. Even fast download speeds feel slow with high ping.
The variation in ping times between packets. Consistent timing means a more stable and predictable connection.
Under 5 ms is excellent. 5–20 ms is acceptable. Above 30 ms indicates connection instability.
High jitter causes choppy audio in calls, buffering in streams, and rubber-banding in games, even when average speeds look fine.
Below 90% health means your battery has lost some of its original capacity. This is normal aging — most batteries degrade to 80% within 2–3 years. You may notice shorter battery life between charges.
Below 80% health, the fuel gauge becomes less accurate. Your phone may shut down at 5–15% because the remaining capacity isn\'t usable. This is a sign the battery should be replaced.
Keeping your battery between 20–80% and avoiding overnight full charges helps extend its lifespan. Heat during charging is the biggest enemy — remove thick cases when charging.
Your battery is draining faster than normal with the screen off. This usually means a background app is keeping your phone awake. Check App Usage to identify the culprit.
Why your phone slows down when it\'s hot

Your phone intentionally reduces CPU and GPU speed when temperature rises to prevent hardware damage. This protects your phone but makes it feel sluggish. Move to a cooler environment and close intensive apps.
Heat and battery wear compound each other
High temperature accelerates battery chemical degradation, and a degraded battery generates more heat under load. This creates a cycle. If your phone is consistently warm, it will age faster.

When signal is poor, your phone boosts its radio transmission power to maintain the connection. This can use 2–3 times more battery than a strong signal. Consider moving closer to your router or a window.

Speed tests measure your connection to one server at one moment. Real-world performance varies by time of day, server distance, and network congestion. Run multiple tests at different times for a better picture.
Why a full phone is a slow phone
When storage is above 75% full, your phone has less room for temporary files, app caches, and system operations. This can slow down app launches, photo saving, and even system updates.

Apps and their data usually use the most space, followed by photos and videos. System files, cached data, and downloaded files can also add up. The media breakdown above shows your personal usage pattern.
What battery health really means, how cycle count affects it, and when to consider a replacement.

## What is battery health?

Battery health measures how much of your battery's original capacity remains. A new phone starts at 100%. Over time, the chemical processes inside the lithium-ion cell become less efficient, and the maximum charge it can hold decreases.

## How cycle count affects health

A charge cycle is one full discharge of the battery's capacity — but it doesn't have to happen all at once. Using 50% today and 50% tomorrow equals one cycle. Most phone batteries are rated for 500–800 cycles before reaching 80% health.

## When to replace your battery

Below 80% health, you'll notice significantly shorter battery life. Below 70%, unexpected shutdowns become common because the fuel gauge can't accurately measure remaining charge. Most manufacturers recommend replacement around 80%.
What causes high drain, how to read drain rates, and practical tips to extend battery life.

## What is drain rate?

Drain rate measures how fast your battery percentage is dropping, usually expressed as percent per hour (%/h). Light use might show 0.5–1%/h, while gaming or navigation can hit 10%/h or more.

## Common drain causes

The screen is the biggest power consumer, followed by cellular radio (especially with weak signal), GPS, and CPU-intensive apps. Background processes like sync, location services, and push notifications also contribute.

## Screen on vs. screen off

Comparing screen-on and screen-off drain rates helps identify problems. If your phone drains fast with the screen off, a background app is likely keeping it awake. Check the deep sleep analysis to see if your phone is entering Doze mode properly.
Separate fact from fiction about charging habits and learn what actually extends battery life.

## Myth: You should fully discharge before charging

False. Lithium-ion batteries actually prefer partial charges. Deep discharges (below 20%) stress the battery more than topping up from 50%.

## Myth: Overnight charging damages your battery

Mostly false for modern phones. Most devices stop charging at 100% and trickle-charge to maintain it. However, keeping the battery at 100% in a warm environment does accelerate degradation.

## What actually helps

Keep your battery between 20–80% when practical. Avoid heat during charging — remove cases, don't charge in direct sunlight. Use the manufacturer's charger or a certified one. Slower charging generates less heat and is gentler on the battery.
What milliamps and watts mean for your battery, and how to interpret these readings.

## Current (mA)

Current measures the flow of electricity in milliamps. When discharging, it's negative (power flowing out). When charging, it's positive (power flowing in). Higher absolute values mean more power is being used or delivered.

## Power (W)

Power in watts equals voltage times current (V × A). Watts give a more accurate picture of energy use because they account for voltage changes. A phone using 500 mA at 3.7 V uses about 1.85 W.

## Reading accuracy

Not all phones report current accurately. Some show instantaneous readings that fluctuate rapidly, others show smoothed averages. The confidence badge in runcheck tells you how reliable your device's readings are. Use the average and min/max stats for a better picture than any single reading.
What temperatures are safe, when to worry, and how your phone manages heat.

## What's normal?

A phone at rest should be near room temperature (20–25 °C). Under light use, 25–35 °C is typical. During gaming or camera use, 35–42 °C is common and acceptable.

## When to worry

Above 45 °C during normal use is concerning. If your phone feels uncomfortably hot to touch, it's likely above 40 °C. The battery temperature sensor gives you an accurate reading rather than relying on feel.

## How your phone manages heat

Android monitors multiple thermal zones across the device. When temperatures rise, it first reduces background work, then limits CPU/GPU frequencies (throttling), and in extreme cases may display a thermal warning or shut down to prevent damage.
Why your phone slows down when hot, what the throttling levels mean, and how to minimize it.

## What is throttling?

Thermal throttling is your phone intentionally reducing processor speed to control heat. It's a safety mechanism that protects your hardware from damage.

## Throttling levels

Android defines several levels: None (full speed), Light (minor reduction), Moderate (noticeable), Severe (significant impact), Critical (emergency). Most daily throttling stays at Light or Moderate.

## Thermal headroom

The headroom value shows how close you are to throttling. Think of it as a temperature budget — when it reaches zero, throttling kicks in. Monitoring headroom helps predict performance dips before they happen.

## Reducing throttling

Avoid using intensive apps while charging. Remove phone cases that trap heat. Reduce screen brightness. In hot environments, give your phone breaks between demanding tasks.
How heat damages batteries and how damaged batteries create more heat.

## The cycle

High temperatures accelerate battery chemical degradation. A degraded battery has higher internal resistance. Higher resistance means more energy is wasted as heat during charging and discharging. More heat means faster degradation.

## Numbers that matter

At 25 °C, a battery might last 500 cycles to 80% health. At 35 °C, that might drop to 300 cycles. At 45 °C, even less. Each 10 °C increase roughly doubles the rate of chemical aging.

## Breaking the cycle

Avoid charging in hot environments. Don't use demanding apps while charging. If your phone gets hot frequently, check for misbehaving apps. Consider replacing the battery if health is below 80% and the phone runs warm — the old battery is likely contributing to the heat problem.
What dBm means, how to read signal strength, and why it matters more than bars.

## Why dBm instead of bars?

Signal bars are simplified and vary by manufacturer — four bars on one phone might show three bars on another. dBm (decibels relative to one milliwatt) is the actual measurement, giving you a consistent and accurate reading.

## Reading dBm values

dBm values are negative — closer to zero means stronger. The scale is logarithmic: every 3 dBm change doubles or halves the signal power. So -70 dBm is ten times stronger than -80 dBm.

## Signal quality ranges

For Wi-Fi: -30 to -50 dBm is excellent, -50 to -70 dBm is good, -70 to -80 dBm is weak, below -80 dBm is poor. For cellular: -80 to -100 dBm is good, -100 to -110 dBm is fair, below -110 dBm is poor.

## Battery impact

Weak signals force your phone to increase radio transmission power, draining battery faster. Moving to a location with better signal can noticeably improve battery life.
2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz vs 6 GHz, Wi-Fi generations, and choosing the right connection.

## Frequency bands

2.4 GHz travels farther and penetrates walls better, but is slower and more congested. 5 GHz is faster with less interference but shorter range. 6 GHz (Wi-Fi 6E) offers the best speeds and least congestion but requires newer devices and has the shortest range.

## Wi-Fi standards

Wi-Fi 4 (802.11n): up to 600 Mbps, introduced in 2009. Wi-Fi 5 (802.11ac): up to 3.5 Gbps, 5 GHz only. Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax): up to 9.6 Gbps, both bands, better in crowded environments. Wi-Fi 6E: same as Wi-Fi 6 plus 6 GHz band.

## Practical tips

Connect to 5 GHz when near your router for fastest speeds. Use 2.4 GHz when you need range through walls. Your phone usually auto-selects the best band, but you can separate network names in your router settings for manual control.
What speed tests measure, their limitations, and how to get meaningful results.

## What they measure

A speed test sends and receives data to a test server, measuring: download speed (how fast you receive), upload speed (how fast you send), ping (round-trip latency), and jitter (latency variation).

## Limitations

Results reflect the path between your phone and one specific server at one specific moment. Results vary by: time of day (networks are slower during peak hours), server distance, Wi-Fi vs cellular, other devices using your network, and background apps.

## Getting useful results

Run tests at different times of day. Close other apps first. Test on both Wi-Fi and cellular. Compare multiple results rather than relying on a single test. Use the history in runcheck to track trends over time.
Why a nearly-full phone runs slower and what you can do about it.

## How storage affects speed

Modern flash storage (UFS/eMMC) needs free space to operate efficiently. The controller uses free blocks for wear leveling, garbage collection, and write amplification management. Below about 25% free space, these operations become constrained.

## What you'll notice

App launches take longer. Photos take a moment to save. System updates may fail. Apps may crash when they can't write temporary data. The overall system feels sluggish.

## Fixing it

Start with the cleanup tools in runcheck — large files, old downloads, and APK installers are easy wins. Review apps you haven't used recently. Move photos to cloud storage. Clear app caches (social media apps are often the biggest offenders).
Understanding what uses your storage and how to manage each category effectively.

## The main categories

Apps and their data typically use the most space (30–60% on most phones). Photos and videos are usually second. System files take 10–15 GB that you can't reclaim. Downloads, documents, and audio make up the rest.

## Hidden storage consumers

App caches grow over time — social media apps can cache gigabytes of images and videos. Messaging apps store all received media. Offline maps and music can use significant space. Old APK installer files linger in Downloads.

## Management strategies

Regularly review large files (runcheck can find these). Set messaging apps to not auto-download media. Use streaming instead of offline downloads when storage is tight. The media breakdown chart in runcheck shows exactly where your space is going.
How runcheck calculates the overall health score from battery, network, thermal, and storage data.

## What it measures

The health score combines four categories: battery (level, health, drain rate), network (signal quality, latency), thermal (temperature, headroom, throttling), and storage (available space, fill rate).

## How it's calculated

Each category produces a 0–100 sub-score based on current readings compared to ideal ranges. These are weighted and combined into the overall score. Battery and thermal are weighted slightly higher because they affect the entire device experience.

## Score ranges

90–100: Excellent — your device is in great shape. 70–89: Good — normal wear and minor issues. 50–69: Fair — noticeable issues that could be addressed. Below 50: Poor — significant problems affecting daily use.

## Improving your score

The score highlights which category needs attention. Check the detail screen for each category to see specific recommendations. Some factors (like battery age) can't be improved without hardware changes, but many others (like clearing storage or fixing signal issues) can be addressed easily.
How to tell if your phone\'s issues are from software (fixable) or hardware (needs repair).

## Software problems

Software issues are usually fixable without repair: high drain from a misbehaving app, slow performance from full storage, poor network from bad settings, overheating from a stuck background process. These often appear suddenly after an app update or setting change.

## Hardware problems

Hardware issues require physical repair or replacement: battery health below 80% (chemistry degraded), persistent overheating at idle (thermal paste failure or chip degradation), Wi-Fi that disconnects regardless of router (antenna issue).

## How to tell the difference

Use runcheck's data over time. Software problems often show sudden changes — drain rate spikes after installing an app, or temperature jumps after an OS update. Hardware problems show gradual trends — battery health slowly declining, thermal performance consistently worsening.

## What to do

For software: try safe mode to rule out apps, clear caches, check for OS updates. For hardware: check warranty status, visit an authorized repair center. Battery replacement is usually affordable and makes the biggest difference for aging phones.
